content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Looking for a movie where the villain is actually hyper-competent and terrifyingly smart, not just "evil and crazy."

**Score:** 152
**Body:** I'm tired of villains who fail because of a stupid monologue or a random mistake. I want a movie like *No Country for Old Men* (Anton Chigurh) or *The Dark Knight* (Joker), where the antagonist feels like an unstoppable force of nature because they are genuinely smarter than everyone else in the room.

Any genre is fine, as long as the cat-and-mouse game feels high stakes.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. In the Line of Fire (1993)


John Malkovich plays a smart and very capable villain & is a ton of fun to watch
  - See also: Malkovich in Con Air.
2. Hans Gruber (Die Hard)
3. I imagine you've seen it but Silence Of The Lambs fits the bill. 


Cape Fear (the remake from the same year; 1991) probably too. But bear in mind there is a 'villain error,' albeit naturally induced. 


You could try Wolf Creek too, a movie from my home country of Australia. I couldn't get past how shit the script was and turned it off after fifteen minutes, but you might be different lol. The villain in it has become something of a movie icon and is one scary motherfucker, particularly if you've had any experience with those remote backwater towns
4. Watchmen
  - Do you seriously think >!I'd explain my master-stroke if there remained the slightest chance of you affecting its outcome?!<
5. Law Abiding Citizen
6. Forget which Mission Impossible it is where Philip Seymour Hoffman plays the villain, but he’s really great as a genius supervillain in an otherwise color-by-numbers, somewhat incomprehensible action shlock flick.
7. Inglorious Basterds - Christoff Waltz character "Hans Landa" is terrifyingly competent and REALLY good at his job.
8. Seven

## 2. Seeking a rare deeply meaningful film that is not commonly suggested

**Score:** 90
**Body:** I am in the mood for a movie that offers depth, reflection and a sense of quiet wisdom. I have already watched most of the famous recommendations that usually come up in these discussions, so I would really value suggestions that are underrated or not talked about as much. Any era or country is fine. Thanks.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Harold and Maude. Masterpiece of a film. Quirky and intelligent from the 1970 s.
2. Ghost Dog: Way of the Samurai
3. Being There
 starring Peter Sellers and Shirley MacLaine. It's a quiet, poignant film with a low key laugh here and there and a beautiful tale. 95 rating on Rotten Tomatoes.
4. Brooklyn with Saoirse Ronan
5. Perfect Days

The Straight Story

Lucky (2017)

Ikiru

Never Cry Wolf

Local Hero

Cinema Paradiso

Paris, Texas

Wings of Desire

A River Runs Through It

Past Lives
  - Upvote for Perfect Days
6. for me it's Cloud Atlas. really makes me think about how peoples' individual choices impact the next generation and so on. I don't know if I'd say the film has quiet wisdom, but there's certainly a lot to chew on.
7. Kundun, The Last Picture Show, Raising Arizona (I’m not joking)
8. Tokyo Story
9. Local Hero

## 3. Looking for movies with great song-based soundtracks (not instrumental scores)

**Score:** 67
**Body:** Hi everyone. I’m looking for movies with great soundtracks made mostly of songs with lyrics by various artists. I’m not looking for orchestral scores or instrumental music written for the film. I prefer soundtracks that use existing songs, or songs recorded by artists that feel like real tracks you’d listen to outside the movie. Any genre or era is fine. Thanks in advance for the suggestions.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Trainspotting is an absolute beauty.
2. Almost Famous
3. The Blues Brothers
4. The Crow
5. Singles and Hi Fidelity
6. Dazed and Confused
7. *”Good Morning Vietnam”* (1987)

*”The Big Chill”* (1983)

*”Forrest Gump”* (1994)

*”The Commitments”* (1991)
8. Stand by Me.
9. O Brother Where Art Thou
10. Guardians of the Galaxy revitalized the interest in many of the songs it featured.

## 4. Call me by your name

**Score:** 24
**Body:** I absolutely love this movie, are there any more that are kind of European summer romantic vibe? Doesn’t even have to be a romance just something beautiful, soft, and scenic like that. I love the music in it as well! Thank you! 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. la piscine
2. The Talented Mr. Ripley (1999)
  - At least it checks OP’s Europe, summer and romance requirements.
3. A Good Year
4. Under the Tuscan Sun?
5. I think Bernardo Bertolucci has some films with this vibe. I'd start with *Stealing Beauty* specifically, but *The Dreamers & The Sheltering Sky* are good too. 

Maybe also: *Y tu mama tambien, Sex and Lucia, & Vicky Christina Barcelona. Portrait of a Lady on Fire* is amazing, but not exactly summertime. 

And Richard Linklater's "Before" trilogy might fit some of this - especially the 1st one.
6. Conte d’été - (Éric Rohmer) many years since I watched it. Also The Big Blue (Luc Besson)
7. The Hand of God (Italian)
8. Worst Person In The World, Before Sunrise
9. A Bigger Splash. Same director, less romantic, more love-triangle/sexual rivals type thing. I find all of his movies romantic in a way, at least they're all love letters to a certain lifestyle/setting. Bones and All, Challengers too, to a lesser extent.

## 5. i believe sing sing is one of the best a24 movies and i never see anyone talk about it

**Score:** 18
**Body:** the criminal justice club at my college held a movie night playing this and i went into it knowing almost nothing and left with a new favorite movie and everything i’ve learned about it since has just made it better.

it’s based on the real life rehabilitation through the arts program in sing sing prison and it stars a lot of real formerly incarcerated alumni of that program just portraying themselves which i think is really cool! and as someone who has watched a good amount of media about prisons, i think this movie does the best at humanizing it’s characters likely because of this.

i will admit i am a legal studies major and in general i have an interest in college/arts in prison programs so i am probably biased. but i just love this movie and i want more people to love it too

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. A couple of the inmate actors are great! 

I missed a free ticket promo when it was in theaters, I wonder how many promotions like this are out there, Netflix did a cheap 4-film package in October.
  - They were mostly prison actors except for the main actor and the other guy from Sound of Metal
2. I love how they incorporated ALL the ideas into the play. it's such a celebration of collaboration and the prioritizing the process of making art over the result
  - yess they were all just there to uplift each other ☹️
3. It was a good movie and niche prison movie regarding the theatrical arts like the movie Weeds with Nick Nolte which is the only other comparable movie of its niche genre. I enjoyed Sing Sing a lot and it was recognized by the Academy awards.
4. I agree. It's underrated. I love movies about the artistic process, and this one ranks high on my list.
5. Best movie from 2023
6. I don't believe A24 is capable of making a good movie.
  - boo 🗣️🗣️

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Name a scene from a movie that has been etched into your memory from the moment you saw it.

**Score:** 263
**Body:** Whenever I see wooden logs or anything being carried by a truck on the highway, this scene comes to my mind. It is deeply etched into my memory.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. If you ever find a movie called Honeymoon in your parent’s closet, believe me, do not watch.
  - Well that’s one way to destroy one’s innocence.
2. Bone Tomahawk , most people will know the scene who've seen the movie
  - I find that people are generally divided on that film
3. I can’t in all honesty say The Butterfly Effect is a good movie…but there’s at least two scenes that have that stuck with me.

The scene where Eric Stoltz is filming the kids and the scene where they put a small explosive in the ladie’s mailbox.
4. The curb stomping scene in American History X.
5. Girl Next Door basement scene
6. I will never drive behind a log truck because of this movie.
7. I watched Misery all they way through for the first time recently.

I was not prepared for \_that\_ scene 🔨 🦵
8. Misery, the hobbling scene.

## 2. Empire Records (1995). I just love this mid-rated movie so much

**Score:** 215
**Body:** Cast, soundtrack, quotes, 90's DNA... Everything about this movie makes me happy

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I don't feel that I need to explain my art to you, Warren.
  - What's with today, today?
2. Super quotable, too.

“Sinead O’Rebellion.” 😆
  - My favourite line is always this:

Marc answers the phone answers and says

"Empire Records, open till midnight"

(Waits a beat then says)

"MIDNIGHT!" and hangs up.
  - Shock me shock me shock me with that deviant behaviour!
3. Such a great 90s film. Rex Manning day
4. One of my all-time faves
5. This movie made me want to work in a record store… specifically with Liv Tyler and Renee Zellweger.
6. "I dont feel that I need to explain my art to you Warren."
7. SHOPLIFTER!!!!

I loved the soundtrack.  I'd bought the CD and worn it out before I ever saw the movie, and then I loved the movie!

## 3. Female actresses who deserved to win the Oscar for Best Actress.

**Score:** 169
**Body:** I've already seen the films that were competing with these actresses for the Best Actress. That's my opinion, and they deserve more than those who actually won, even though this list includes an actress who wasn't even nominated, in this case Isabelle Adjani for Possession. This is my list.

 * Dorothy Dandridge - Carmen Jones (1955)

 * Angela Bassett - What's Love Got To Do With It (1994)

 * Jessica Lange - Frances (1983)

 * Isabelle Adjani - Possession (1982)

 * Deborah Kerr - Heaven Knows, Mr. Allison (1958)

 * Fernanda Montenegro - Central Station (1999)

 * Gabourey Sidibe - Precious (2010)

 * Rosamund Pike - Gone Girl (2015)

 * Fernanda Torres - I'm Still Here (2025)

Simply phenomenal performances, actresses at their best, and they deserved the recognition for it.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Toni Collette for Hereditary
  - Absolutely one of the biggest Oscar snubs in history.
2. Bitch is this cake?
3. * Both Whoopi Goldberg and Oprah Winfrey should have won for The Colour Purple. The fact that the movie was nominated for SO MANY Oscars and didn't get any was bullshit.

* Ruth Gordon in Harold and Maude.

* Ellen Burstyn in Requiem for a Dream ABSOLUTELY should have won over fucking Julia Roberts in Erin Brockovich, ugh.
  - I will die on the Ellen Burstyn hill. One of the best monologues ever.
    - There's a lot to be said that the film goes slightly out of frame because the cameraman was crying and couldn't see through the tears
  - burstyn’s performance in requiem is the main reason i’ve ever rewatched that movie
4. Maggie Cheung - In The Mood For Love (2000)
5. Angela Basset got robbed for not winning when she was nominated for playing Tina Turner. Her and Denzel not winning for Malcolm X are the biggest robberies.
6. I don’t think she was even nominated, but Pam Grier in Jackie Brown. Also agree about Rosamund Pike.

## 4. Phenomenal performance you didn't expect.

**Score:** 111
**Body:** For me it's Kevin Spacey in The usual suspects.
Absolutely fun and fantastic movie. 

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Kevin spacey is unfortunately an excellent actor 
  - Drives me absolutely insane, I loved his work so much
    - Verbal Kint, Jack Vincennes were two of my favorites. I still enjoy those movies but with a bit of reservation now.
      - LA Confidential is such a masterwork there’s so many other great things to focus on. That doesn’t entirely take away what a bummer it is, but it helps.
  - Yeah he's amazing in every movie
2. Brad Pitt - 12 Monkeys. 

I’d totally written him off as a pretty boy with average acting skills. 

Everyone was fantastic in that movie. 
  - He's also off the hook good in Burn After Reading. Great comedic chops
  - Watch kalifornia next
3. Heath ledger, dark knight
  - Fr. The Brokeback Mountain guy!? SERIOUSLY!? Everyone HATED his casting until the trailers came out.  

Who knew he’d create a cinematic icon.

## 5. Do you believe Forrest was not that intelligent or was he bright enough to know, and autistic?

**Score:** 95
**Body:** This line of reasoning is a common philosophical paradox that suggests that the self-awareness required to recognize one's own limitations is, in itself, a form of intelligence. Therefore, if a person accurately believes they are "stupid," they are, by the very act of that accurate assessment, not entirely stupid. 
 
 





### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. When he first meets his child. The first thing he asks Jenny is “is he like me?” In tears. That means that throughout the entire movie, everything he experienced, every insult and hardship, he knew and internalized. He feels fear that his kid would suffer like he did, that implies some level of self awareness and perhaps shame for his condition, but through it all he earned a lifetime of achievement, success, and love from everyone he met
  - Also when Bubba dies he says how he lost his best good friend and how even he knows something like that doesn’t just come around the corner. Then of course the I’m not a smart man, but I do know what love is scene.
  - He spent his whole life afraid he wasn't enough... and still gave the world everything. 😢
  - Zemekis with the shamalan twist! Fr though that scene hit like a bus. Re-contextualizes the entire film from happy go lucky to heart wrenching pity. Or something it's hard to put into words. Maybe the Germans have a term for it. But I guess that's why we have art and music.
2. He was simple. And this simplicity is what made him successful.  
  - Simple is as simple does.
  - This!!! I think to put it most lovelingly is that he was literally a "simpleton" (eventhough the definition might point to lack of intellegence)
Hes simple in the way he see things for what they are. He doesnt overthink, and isn't bother by aniticipatory anxeity. He does what he thinks is good and right - and does it despite other people say otherwise or even under enemy fire!

Thats why we love him!
3. Forrest lacks obvious social cues, misunderstands basic concepts and demonstrates only rudimentary comprehension skills, yet he ultimately thrives over the course of his lifetime. The movie portrays a lot of his good fortune as accidental, serendipitous and dumb luck.

Yet, he possesses an indomitable personality and spirit, unshaken by hardship, war, heartbreak or personal tragedies, that his success also seems to be guaranteed. His naivety and aloofness become an armour that makes him unbreakable to whatever hardships life throws at him.
  - Good assessment! Also worth noting that in the book he was apparently much more of a savant. He aces an advanced physics class that he took by accident. The movie toned it down cuz i guess its a bit cliche
4. Sorry if this is rambling, I have a lot of thoughts about this.

Forrest knew enough to know that he wasn’t like other people and I believe he probably heard how stupid he was his whole life.   From what I’ve seen of my autistic daughter as she grow, she’ll rise to the lowest expectation of them regardless of what she’s actually capable of.  Their first year in a mainstream school was hell, until I managed to convince her teacher to challenge her.  

If you rewatch [the scene where he meets Forrest Jnr,](https://youtu.be/WHSMqbQ2bks?si=tjkg_jvxCqNpKxbi)  he starts reacting while saying ‘He got a daddy named Forrest too?’  which leads me to believe that he caught Jenny’s implication before she spelled it out for him.   

Then the heartbreaking moment of ‘Is he smart or-‘ tells us that Forrest absolutely knows he has intellectual struggles.  Whether the depth of them is accurate or if he’s only risen to the lowest expectation & parroted what he’s been told his whole life, I have no idea.   

My theory is that he is self aware & intelligent enough to know that he’s different to those around him, while still having a lower than average IQ.  If nobody has ever pushed him, or challenged him in the right way, (and consider the times in which the movie is set) then how can we say one way or the other?

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

---
